# Google AI Studio Prompt: Dr. Jonathan Rasouli Website Content Generation

## Context & Instructions

You are tasked with creating professional, comprehensive website content for **Dr. Jonathan J. Rasouli, MD**, a board-certified, dual fellowship-trained neurosurgeon specializing in complex and adult spinal deformity surgery. Generate four distinct pages based on the detailed information provided below.

---

## BACKGROUND INFORMATION

### Professional Profile
**Dr. Jonathan J. Rasouli, MD** is a New York-based neurosurgeon at Northwell Health - Staten Island University Hospital and NYC Health + Hospitals/South Brooklyn Health. He is recognized as an emerging leader in neurosurgery with extensive expertise in:
- Complex and adult spinal deformity surgery
- Minimally invasive spine surgery
- Robotic spine surgery
- Artificial intelligence applications in spine surgery
- Stereotactic and functional neurosurgery

### Education & Training
- **Medical School**: Albert Einstein College of Medicine (2007-2012)
  - Graduated with honors
  - Inducted into Alpha Omega Alpha Medical Honor Society
  - Received Research Distinction in Neurosurgery
  
- **Residency**: Mount Sinai Hospital and Medical Center (2012-2019)
  - Neurological Surgery
  - Served as Chief Resident
  - Completed enfolded fellowship in Stereotactic and Functional Neurosurgery under Dr. Brian Kopell

- **Fellowship**: Cleveland Clinic Foundation (2019-2020)
  - Combined Neurosurgery and Orthopaedic Spine Surgery Fellowship
  - Promoted to Assistant Professor of Neurosurgery at Cleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine
  - Advanced minimally invasive and robotic spine surgery programs

### Board Certification
- American Board of Neurological Surgery (2023)

### Current Positions
- Assistant Professor of Neurosurgery, Donald and Barbara Zucker School of Medicine at Hofstra/Northwell
- Neurosurgeon, Northwell Health - Staten Island University Hospital
- Neurosurgeon, NYC Health + Hospitals/South Brooklyn Health, Ruth Bader Ginsburg Hospital

### Languages
- English (fluent)
- Farsi (fluent)

### Recognition & Awards
- **Castle Connolly Rising Star (2023)** - Recognized for outstanding accomplishments and dedication to medicine, emerging leadership, and contributions to healthcare advancement
- Sue B. Golding Service & Research Scholarship (2007)
- Featured in major media outlets: CNN, NY Post, The Washington Post, Becker's Spine

### Research & Publications
Dr. Rasouli has authored numerous peer-reviewed articles and book chapters (cited over 1,200 times). Key research areas include:
- Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Spine Surgery
- Robotic Surgery Applications
- Minimally Invasive Spine Techniques
- Virtual Education Platforms (Virtual Global Spine Conference)
- Blockchain Technology in Healthcare Infrastructure
- Optimization of Spine Surgery Outcomes
- Surgical Site Infection Prevention
- Traumatic Brain Injury Treatment
- Pulsed Electromagnetic Field Therapy

**Notable Publications:**
1. "Artificial Intelligence and Robotics in Spine Surgery" - Global Spine Journal (2021)
2. "Assessing the Accessibility, Engagement, and Value of the Virtual Global Spine Conference as an Educational Platform" - World Neurosurgery (2024)
3. "Editorial: Optimization of spine surgery outcomes in the pre-, peri-, and postoperative settings" - Frontiers in Surgery (2023)
4. "Negative Pressure Wound Therapy in Spinal Surgery" - Bioengineering (2022)
5. "Emerging Blockchain Technology Solutions for Modern Healthcare Infrastructure" - Journal of Scientific Innovation in Medicine (2019)
6. "Virtual Spine: A Novel, International Teleconferencing Program Developed to Increase the Accessibility of Spine Education During the COVID-19 Pandemic"
7. "Disparities in Outcomes by Insurance Payer Groups for Patients Undergoing Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion"

### Professional Memberships & Committees
- **Young Neurosurgeons Committee** - Member (2020-Present)
- **NASS Political Engagement Committee** - Member (2020-Present)
- **Congress of Neurological Surgeons Resident Fellow Committee** - Resident Fellow (2018-2020)

### Conference Presentations
Dr. Rasouli has presented at numerous national and international conferences:

- Virtual Global Spine Conference (founding organizer and presenter)

### Clinical Expertise
**Surgical Specialties:**
- Complex spinal deformity correction

**Technology Integration:**

### Educational Initiatives
Dr. Rasouli is passionate about medical education and has:
- Co-founded the Virtual Global Spine Conference during COVID-19 pandemic
- Developed international teleconferencing programs for spine education
- Created educational content for medical students and trainees
- Built social media platforms for neurosurgical education and collaboration
- Authored educational book chapters on surgical techniques

### Philosophy & Approach
Dr. Rasouli combines cutting-edge technology with compassionate patient care. His approach emphasizes:
- Evidence-based medicine
- Minimally invasive techniques when appropriate
- Patient-centered decision making
- Continuous innovation and research
- Collaborative care with multidisciplinary teams
- Global education and knowledge sharing

---

## PAGE 1: STATEMENT PAGE

### Instructions for AI
Create a compelling "About Dr. Rasouli" or "Statement" page that introduces visitors to Dr. Rasouli's mission, vision, and approach to neurosurgery. This should be written in first person, reflecting his voice as both a compassionate physician and innovative researcher.

### Instructions for AI

## PAGE 4: BLOG PAGE

### Instructions for AI
Create 5 distinct blog post outlines/drafts that showcase Dr. Rasouli's expertise, thought leadership, and commitment to patient education. Each post should be informative, accessible to general audiences, and reflect current topics in neurosurgery.

### Blog Post #1: "The Future is Now: How AI and Robotics are Transforming Spine Surgery"

### Blog Post #4: "Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery: Separating Fact from Fiction"

**Content:**
- Define minimally invasive spine surgery

- Real benefits: smaller incisions, less tissue damage, faster recovery
- Common myths and misconceptions

**Length**: 800-1,000 words
**Tone**: Honest, educational, myth-busting

---

### Blog Post #5: "A Day in the Life: What Really Happens During Spine Surgery"

**Content:**
- Pre-operative preparation and patient safety protocols

- Technology in the operating room (navigation, robotics, imaging)
- Decision-making during surgery
- What patients experience (anesthesia, positioning, monitoring)

- Why each case is unique
- Patient stories (anonymized) and outcomes
- The privilege of being a surgeon

**Length**: 900-1,100 words
**Tone**: Behind-the-scenes, humanizing, inspiring

---

## GENERAL FORMATTING GUIDELINES

### For All Pages:
1. **Use clear section headers** with proper HTML heading hierarchy (H1, H2, H3)
2. **Include keywords naturally**: neurosurgery, spine surgery, spinal deformity, minimally invasive, robotic surgery, AI in medicine, New York neurosurgeon
3. **Write in accessible language** - avoid excessive medical jargon or define terms when used
4. **Include calls-to-action** where appropriate (schedule consultation, learn more, contact office)
5. **Maintain professional yet warm tone** throughout
6. **Use short paragraphs** (3-5 sentences) for web readability
7. **Incorporate bullet points** where appropriate for scannability
8. **Add transition sentences** between sections for flow

### SEO Considerations:
- Primary keywords: Dr. Jonathan Rasouli, spine surgeon, neurosurgeon New York, spinal deformity surgery, minimally invasive spine surgery, robotic spine surgery
- Secondary keywords: Staten Island neurosurgeon, Brooklyn spine surgery, AI in spine surgery, complex spine surgery, vertebral surgery
- Include location markers: Staten Island, Brooklyn, New York City, Northwell Health

### Accessibility:
- Use descriptive language for screen readers
- Clear heading structure
- Readable font sizes and spacing
- High contrast text

---

## ADDITIONAL NOTES

**Research Citations**: When referencing specific studies or publications, use general language ("research has shown," "studies indicate," "in a recent publication") rather than specific citations unless listing publications in the CV section.

**Patient Privacy**: Do not create specific patient stories or testimonials - these should be general examples only.

**Medical Accuracy**: Maintain medical accuracy while keeping content accessible to general audiences. Avoid making guarantees about surgical outcomes.

**Professional Image**: Content should reflect Dr. Rasouli's status as both a skilled clinician and innovative researcher, while remaining humble and patient-focused.

**Cultural Sensitivity**: Acknowledge diverse patient populations he serves through his work at public hospitals and language skills.

1. Page title

4. Suggested imagery/visual elements
5. Call-to-action elements

Begin with the Statement page, followed by Credentials/CV, Global Humanitarian Work, and finally the 5 blog posts.

import React from 'react'; import { Link } from 'react-router-dom'; import Button from '../components/Button'; import { Quote, Heart, Brain, Globe } from 'lucide-react'; const Statement: React.FC = () => { return (
{/* Hero Banner */}

A Surgeon's Promise

"My commitment is to treat every patient with the same level of care, precision, and compassion I would want for my own family."

{/* Journey Section */}

My Journey to Neurosurgery

My path to becoming a neurosurgeon began not just with a fascination for the complexities of the human nervous system, but with a deep-seated desire to make a tangible difference in people's lives. Starting with a background in biomedical engineering, I learned to see the spine not just as anatomy, but as an elegant, mechanical structure that supports our entire way of life.

During my medical training at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and my residency at Mount Sinai, I witnessed firsthand how debilitating spinal conditions can be. I saw patients losing their mobility, their independence, and their hope. It was then that I knew I had to dedicate my career to mastering the most advanced techniques in spinal surgery.

The privilege of being a surgeon is immense. Patients entrust us with their bodies and their futures. I pursued dual fellowship training in both neurosurgery and orthopaedic spine surgery at the Cleveland Clinic to ensure I could offer my patients the most comprehensive, expert care possible—viewing every problem from multiple angles to find the perfect solution.

{/* Philosophy Section */}

Philosophy of Care

I believe that you, the patient, are the most important member of the care team. My philosophy is rooted in a partnership where decisions are made together. I take the time to listen to your story, understand your goals, and explain your condition in plain language.

My approach combines rigorous evidence-based medicine with personalized treatment plans. While I am trained in complex surgical reconstruction, I am also a staunch advocate for conservative care. Surgery should always be the last resort, chosen only when non-surgical methods have been exhausted.

When surgery is necessary, I leverage the latest advancements in minimally invasive techniques and robotic assistance. This technology allows us to achieve precise results with less disruption to the body, leading to faster recovery and less pain. But technology never replaces the human touch; it enhances our ability to care for you.

{/* Future Vision Section */}

Innovation & The Future

We are living in a revolutionary time for spine surgery. The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI), robotics, and augmented reality is transforming what is possible. I am committed to staying at the forefront of this innovation, researching and implementing safe, effective technologies that improve outcomes for my patients.

Beyond the operating room, I am passionate about democratizing access to high-quality spine care. Through initiatives like the Virtual Global Spine Conference, I strive to share knowledge with surgeons around the world, ensuring that advancements in care reach patients everywhere, regardless of geography.

{/* Personal Touch Section */}

What Drives Me

"It is the trust my patients place in me that drives me to be better every single day. Seeing a patient walk into my office pain-free after years of suffering is the greatest reward I can imagine. I am honored to be a part of your journey to recovery."

Jonathan J. Rasouli, MD

Neurosurgeon

{/* CTA */}
); }; export default Statement;

import React from 'react'; import { Globe, Users, Laptop, HeartHandshake, MapPin } from 'lucide-react'; import Button from '../components/Button'; import { Link } from 'react-router-dom'; const GlobalWork: React.FC = () => { return (
{/* Hero */}

Global Humanitarian Impact

Breaking down barriers to spine care education and access through technology, innovation, and international collaboration.

{/* Vision Section */}

Vision for Global Healthcare

I believe that high-quality medical education and healthcare are universal rights, not privileges determined by geography. My vision is a world where a spine surgeon in a remote village has access to the same educational resources as one in a major metropolitan center. By bridging healthcare disparities through technology, we can raise the standard of care for patients everywhere.

{/* Virtual Global Spine Conference */}

The Virtual Global Spine Conference

Origins in Crisis

When the COVID-19 pandemic cancelled major medical conferences worldwide in 2020, the medical community faced an educational void. Seeing an opportunity to connect rather than isolate, I co-founded the Virtual Global Spine Conference. What started as a temporary solution evolved into a revolutionary platform.

Global Reach

Delivered expert case-based education to thousands of healthcare providers across 6 continents, connecting surgeons who had never before collaborated.

Proven Impact

Our three-year review, published in World Neurosurgery (2024), validated the platform's ability to significantly increase accessibility and engagement in spine education.

Ongoing Mission

Today, the conference continues to serve as a vital resource, offering free, accessible continuing medical education and fostering a truly global community of spine specialists.

{/* Tech as a Bridge */}

Technology as a Bridge

Advanced technology isn't just for elite hospitals; it's a tool for equity. Minimally invasive techniques, which I specialize in, are particularly valuable in resource-limited settings because they reduce hospital stays and infection risks.

Furthermore, my research into blockchain and AI aims to create scalable, patient-centric healthcare infrastructures that can be deployed globally to improve surgical outcomes and data security.

{/* Advocacy & Equity */}

Healthcare Equity & Advocacy

My commitment to equity extends to my own community in New York. I actively serve diverse patient populations at NYC Health + Hospitals, treating patients regardless of their background.

Through my work on the NASS Political Engagement Committee and my research into insurance-based disparities in surgical outcomes, I advocate for systemic changes that ensure every patient has access to the best possible spine care. Being fluent in Farsi also allows me to bridge cultural and linguistic gaps for the Persian community, ensuring they feel heard and understood.

{/* Call to Action */}

Join the Global Mission

Whether you are a patient, a colleague, or an advocate, there are ways to support the democratization of healthcare.

); }; export default GlobalWork;

entries.description: Data file for blog posts

diffs.replacement:
export const BLOG_POSTS = [ { slug: 'ai-robotics-spine-surgery', title: "The Future is Now: How AI and Robotics are Transforming Spine Surgery", date: "January 2026", category: "Technology & Innovation", readTime: "5-6 minutes", image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1531746792341-dad4edd7a636?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80", excerpt: "Personal excitement about technological revolution in spine surgery. These aren't distant future concepts – they're being used today to enhance precision and safety.", content: `

The Technological Revolution in Spine Surgery

We are standing at the precipice of a new era in medicine. As a surgeon who specializes in complex spinal deformity, I am incredibly excited about the role Artificial Intelligence (AI) and robotics are playing in our operating rooms today. These are not distant, sci-fi concepts; they are practical tools that I use to improve safety and outcomes for my patients right now.

AI in Preoperative Planning

Before we even enter the operating room, AI algorithms help us analyze MRI and CT scans with unprecedented depth. These systems can recognize patterns in spinal deformities that might be subtle to the human eye, helping us predict how a spine will respond to correction. This predictive modeling allows us to "rehearse" the surgery digitally, ensuring we choose the most effective approach for your unique anatomy.

Robotics: Enhancing Human Skill

Robotic assistance doesn't replace the surgeon; it amplifies our skills. In the operating room, I use robotic arms to guide the placement of instrumentation with sub-millimeter accuracy. This precision is critical in the spine, where a millimeter can mean the difference between a perfect outcome and a complication.

  • Smaller Incisions: Robotics allow for true minimally invasive approaches.
  • Less Radiation: Reduced need for intraoperative X-rays.
  • Faster Recovery: Less tissue disruption means you get back to your life sooner.

The Human Element

Despite these technological marvels, the most important tool remains the surgeon's judgment and the doctor-patient relationship. Technology is a powerful servant but a poor master. I use these tools to execute my surgical plan with greater safety, but the decision to operate and the plan itself are born from listening to you and understanding your goals.

` }, { slug: 'virtual-medicine-lessons', title: "Virtual Medicine: Lessons from the Pandemic That Are Here to Stay", date: "January 2026", category: "Medical Education", readTime: "5 minutes", image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1576091160550-217358c74169?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80", excerpt: "How the Virtual Global Spine Conference changed medical education forever and why hybrid healthcare models benefit patients worldwide.", content: `

Turning Crisis into Opportunity

In March 2020, as the world locked down, the medical conferences we relied on for education were cancelled. Facing this void, I co-founded the Virtual Global Spine Conference. What began as a temporary fix exploded into a global phenomenon, connecting surgeons from over 100 countries.

Breaking Down Barriers

We learned that virtual platforms do more than just replace in-person meetings; they democratize access. Surgeons in developing nations who couldn't afford travel costs were suddenly sitting in the same virtual room as world leaders in neurosurgery. This cross-pollination of ideas improves care for patients globally.

Benefits for Patients

This virtual revolution extends to patient care. Telemedicine has become a permanent, valuable tool in my practice. It allows me to:

  • Consult with patients who live far from New York City.
  • Provide second opinions without requiring travel.
  • Conduct routine follow-ups more conveniently for patients.

The Hybrid Future

While nothing replaces the physical exam, the hybrid model of care is here to stay. It offers the best of both worlds: the convenience of digital connection and the critical nature of in-person care when needed.

` }, { slug: 'understanding-spinal-deformity', title: "Understanding Spinal Deformity: When Should You See a Specialist?", date: "January 2026", category: "Patient Education", readTime: "6-7 minutes", image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1530497610245-94d3c16cda28?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80", excerpt: "Spinal deformity isn't just a cosmetic issue. Learn the warning signs of scoliosis and kyphosis and when it's time to seek expert evaluation.", content: `

More Than Just Posture

Many patients come to me thinking their back pain or posture issues are just part of aging. However, adult spinal deformity—such as scoliosis (side-to-side curve) or kyphosis (forward hunch)—is a progressive medical condition that can significantly impact quality of life.

Warning Signs to Watch For

  • Visible Curvature: Uneven shoulders or hips.
  • Loss of Height: Significant decrease in height over time.
  • Difficulty Standing Upright: Feeling like you are being pulled forward.
  • Pain & Fatigue: Back pain that worsens as the day goes on.

When Surgery is Necessary

Surgery is rarely the first step. We always begin with conservative care like physical therapy. However, surgery becomes a consideration when:

  1. Pain is severe and unremitting.
  2. The deformity is progressing rapidly.
  3. There are neurological symptoms like leg weakness or numbness.
  4. Conservative measures have failed to provide relief.

If you notice these signs, seeing a specialist early is key. Modern surgical techniques allow us to correct deformities that were once considered untreatable, restoring both alignment and quality of life.

` }, { slug: 'minimally-invasive-surgery', title: "Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery: Separating Fact from Fiction", date: "January 2026", category: "Patient Education", readTime: "5-6 minutes", image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1579684385127-1ef15d508118?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80", excerpt: "It sounds too good to be true: less pain, smaller scars, faster recovery. Here is the honest truth about what minimally invasive surgery can (and can't) do.", content: `

What is Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery (MISS)?

MISS is a collection of techniques that allow us to perform surgery through small incisions, using tubular retractors and microscopes to minimize damage to the surrounding muscles. The goal is the same as open surgery—to relieve pressure on nerves or stabilize the spine—but the path to get there is less disruptive.

Myth vs. Reality

Myth: It's "laser surgery" and involves no cutting.

Reality: It is still surgery. We make incisions and remove tissue, but with much greater precision and less collateral damage.

Myth: Everyone is a candidate.

Reality: Not all conditions can be treated this way. Complex deformities often require traditional open approaches to achieve the necessary correction.

The Real Benefits

For the right candidate, MISS offers profound benefits:

  • Significantly less post-operative pain.
  • Lower risk of infection.
  • Less blood loss.
  • Faster return to work and hobbies.

During your consultation, we will determine if MISS is the right tool for your specific condition.

` }, { slug: 'day-in-the-life', title: "A Day in the Life: What Really Happens During Spine Surgery", date: "January 2026", category: "Behind the Scenes", readTime: "6-7 minutes", image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1551601651-2a8555f1a136?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80", excerpt: "Demystifying the operating room. From preoperative planning to the final stitch, join Dr. Rasouli for a look behind the surgical curtain.", content: `

The Preparation

Surgery doesn't start in the OR; it starts days before. I spend the night before every case reviewing imaging, planning every screw placement, and visualizing the procedure. By the time I walk into the hospital, the surgery has already been performed in my mind.

The Team

I am never alone. A spine surgery team is a well-oiled machine including:

  • Anesthesiologists: Keeping you safe and asleep.
  • Neuromonitoring Techs: Watching your nerve function in real-time.
  • Scrub Techs & Nurses: Managing the hundreds of instruments we use.

In the Operating Room

The atmosphere is focused and calm. We use navigation technology that acts like GPS for the spine, allowing us to see exactly where we are in 3D space. Every movement is deliberate. Whether it's a 1-hour discectomy or a 10-hour deformity correction, the goal is the same: perfection.

The Reward

The best part of my day isn't the surgery itself; it's telling a family that their loved one is safe, and later, seeing a patient take their first pain-free steps in the recovery room. That is why I do this.

` } ];
